title: Investment bonds scam targeting Australians with false high-return promises
Fake crypto trading platforms luring investors via WhatsApp and social media groups with no real trading
Mobile number fraud allowing unauthorized account takeover and access to banking services
Romance scams using emotional manipulation to request money and investments from victims
Qantas impersonation scam via email and SMS offering fake rewards and refunds
Spoofed ACCC phone numbers used by scammers impersonating officials requesting personal information
Self-managed super fund scams encouraging illegal early access to retirement savings with promised tax breaks
AI voice cloning scams with deepfakes impersonating family members requesting emergency cash transfers
Fraudulent chargeback scheme targeting small businesses claiming damaged items or non-delivery
Microsoft Teams billing alert scam using guest invitations with fake subscription charge warnings
Fake ANZ verification emails accompanying scam calls requesting banking and personal information
Money laundering threats using cryptocurrency and digital channels to exploit Australian financial system
Community report: scammers impersonating a major telco calling about a 'compromised NBN connection', requesting remote access to the victim's computer to 'fix' it.

Fake crypto platforms target investors through messaging apps and social media. Scammers create convincing fake trading dashboards with no real trading occurring, then request release fees for withdrawals.
Mobile number hijacking scam where criminals take control of phone accounts to receive verification codes and reset passwords, gaining access to financial services and accounts.
Scammers impersonate government agencies like ACCC using spoofed phone numbers to request personal information including passwords and bank details, claiming urgent refunds or penalties.
AI-powered voice and video cloning scams enable fraudsters to impersonate family members and celebrities in emergencies to extract money, or create deepfake investment endorsements.
Scammers pose as loan assistance providers to seasonal workers, targeting those in PALM scheme. They gain banking access under guise of loan application fees then steal deposited funds.
ACMA impersonation phishing emails redirect users to fake data breach pages requesting financial institution selection to harvest credentials and steal banking information.
Illegal early access to superannuation scams exploit SMSF structures. Scammers target users via social media and cold calls, charging large fees while enabling premature fund withdrawals.
Australia Post delivery scams use fake tracking numbers and urgent language claiming parcels cannot be delivered. Users directed to fake websites requesting personal and credit card details.
Fraudulent lottery and prize draw scams exploit social media age restriction confusion introduced December 2025. Criminals pressure users to verify age details through fake websites.
Counterfeit event ticket scams sell fake concert and festival tickets via Facebook and social media. Some events advertised do not exist or have secret location reveals before purchase.

AI-enhanced phishing emails impersonating trusted companies mimic legitimate branding. Generative AI overcomes traditional detection methods by eliminating spelling errors and linguistic inconsistencies.

2025 data shows investment scams topped losses at $837.7 million; payment redirection scams at $166.8 million; and romance scams at $139.9 million. Top five categories accounted for 60% of total losses.
Fake crypto trading platforms targeting Australians through messaging app investment groups, with fabricated dashboards and fake data. Scammers lure victims and charge unlocking fees.
Criminals impersonating legitimate businesses offering fake investment bonds with promised high returns and government protection claims. ASIC has taken down multiple fake websites.
Scammers impersonating Qantas via SMS and emails to steal personal information and money using fake refund and loyalty points offers.
Mobile fraud scam where criminals gain control of phone numbers to receive verification codes and access financial accounts and services.
Scammers spoofing ACCC phone numbers to impersonate agency representatives and request sensitive personal and financial information.
Scammers targeting Australians via social media and cold calls to establish fake self-managed super funds to access retirement savings illegally.
ASIC removing record numbers of scam websites and warns of AI-powered investment scams with fake endorsements and trading bots claiming impossible returns.
Delivery scams impersonating Australia Post asking recipients to confirm details for failed deliveries using fake tracking numbers.
ATO impersonation scam emails demanding immediate disclosure of cryptocurrency holdings or threatening arrest, using false urgency tactics.
Criminals impersonating Australian Federal Police creating fake ReportCyber reports using people's names and emails.
Scammers creating fake ASIC websites mimicking official pages to harvest personal and banking information from unsuspecting users.
Callers impersonating ANZ send fake verification emails with codes to create false legitimacy before attempting unauthorized account transfers.
Fake cryptocurrency trading platforms promoted via messaging apps targeting investors through share trading and stock tips groups with no real trading occurring.
Phone porting scams enable unauthorized access to mobile numbers for identity theft and account takeover targeting victims' 2FA security.
Scammers impersonate AFP and police using fake ReportCyber reports to trick victims into transferring cryptocurrency or sharing seed phrases.
ATO and ASIC warn of SMSF and early superannuation access scams via social media and cold calls encouraging illegal retirement fund withdrawal.
Qantas impersonation scams via SMS and email claiming refunds, gifts or expiring points to steal personal information and funds.
Criminals impersonate real charities on social media stealing donations and personal information through fraudulent fundraising.
ASIC removes record numbers of AI-powered social media phishing and investment scam websites directing consumers to fraudulent platforms.
Recruiters impersonate well-known companies via SMS offering flexible work-from-home opportunities for task-based jobs to steal personal information.
Deepfake and photo-edited advertisements impersonating Australian celebrities and politicians spread via paid social media ads promoting crypto and get-rich schemes.
Superannuation trustees lag significantly behind banks in anti-scam communications with only 40-60% meeting basic fraud protection standards.
Fake invoice scams target small businesses with fraudulent requests for payment on products or services never ordered.
Australians lost $2.18 billion to scams in 2025 with investment scams accounting for $837.7 million and romance scams $139.9 million.
Government announces $3,000 automatic reimbursement scheme for scam victims. Banks and telcos must process claims within 5 days under new Scams Prevention Framework.

ASIC warns of sophisticated fake cryptocurrency trading platforms targeting young investors via social media and messaging apps. Victims lose deposits to fake platforms with no real trading occurring.
ATO and ASIC warn of SMSF early access scams promoted via social media and cold calls. Scammers encourage illegal superannuation withdrawals while charging large fees or taking portions of funds.
NSW authorities report major fraud prosecutions including fake finance applications, rental scams, and employee theft affecting millions of Australians in 2024-25.
AUSTRAC warns of evolving money laundering threats using sophisticated transnational methods. New risks involve illicit tobacco, digital channels, and advanced laundering techniques.
Victorian man sentenced to 2 years 2 months for masterminding phone porting scam targeting mobile phone number theft and identity fraud schemes.
Latest Targeting Scams Report shows $2.18 billion lost to scams in 2025. Investment scams lead losses at $837 million. Top 5 scam types account for 60% of total losses.
ASIC removes record 11,964 phishing and investment scam websites in 2025 (90% increase). AI-powered investment scams increasingly difficult to detect with fake endorsements and deepfakes.
ACCC alerts to spike in Qantas impersonation scams via email and SMS. Scammers create urgency claiming expired points, refunds or gifts to steal login details and payment information.

AFP warns of Operation Firestorm impersonation scam. Criminals pose as AFP cybercrime officers claiming data breach involvement to trick victims into transferring cryptocurrency or sharing wallet seed phrases.
ACCC alerts to phone number spoofing scams impersonating the organisation itself. Scammers demand sensitive information claiming urgency to extract passwords, bank details and personal data.
ASIC finds superannuation funds severely lagging on scam and fraud communications. Only 40-60% meet anti-scam benchmarks compared to banks at 80%+. $22 million lost to super fund scams.
Fake crypto asset trading platforms proliferating through messaging apps. Scammers lure retail investors via WhatsApp and group chats, displaying false profits before stealing deposits.
Mobile number fraud alert from ACMA and Scamwatch. Criminals taking control of phone numbers to intercept verification codes and access bank accounts, myGov, and subscription services.
Qantas impersonation phishing campaign active via email and SMS. Scammers claiming refunds, expiring points or rewards to steal login credentials and personal information.
Cambodian scam centre raid exposes 300 Australians' data. Phone numbers, names and addresses seized in documents, victims at risk of targeted fraud schemes.
Investment bond scam falsely claims ASIC approval. Scammers offering bonds purportedly cancellable after 90 days, targeting retail investors via unsolicited communications.
ACCC phone number spoofing scam. Criminals impersonating ACCC representatives claiming unclaimed refunds or penalties, requesting personal and financial information over phone calls.
Australia Post delivery scam warning. Fake delivery drivers impersonating Australia Post knocking on doors claiming parcels left; real Australia Post never leaves parcels unattended.

AI-powered voice cloning scams escalating. Fraudsters using deepfakes and cloned voices to impersonate family members claiming emergencies, with AU$25.8 million lost to voice scams in first half 2025.
Pacific Australia Labour Mobility scheme workers targeted by fake loan application scams. Fraudsters claiming to assist with loans extract banking details, draining accounts once loan approved.
ATO impersonation scams via email falsely claiming taxable income recalculation and compensation due. Victims requested to provide TFN, payslips and driver's licence for identity theft.
Romance scam statistics show increased losses in Q4 2025. National Anti-Scam Centre taskforce highlights persistent growth in relationship-based fraud targeting vulnerable individuals.

South Australian Seniors Card scam targeting elderly residents. Fake website falsely claiming to assist with card applications for fee; official card always free with no financial information requested.
ASIC warns of fake crypto trading platforms targeting young investors via social media and messaging apps offering trading tips.
AFP reveals sophisticated bank impersonation scams using hold music imitation and tag-team calls to bypass security, draining accounts in hours.
ASIC escalates action against AI-powered investment scams with 24/7 social media monitoring to remove fake trading ads.
ATO and ASIC warn of scams promoting illegal early superannuation access through self-managed super funds with high fees.
Federal government introduces mandatory Scams Prevention Framework requiring banks, telcos, and digital platforms to implement anti-scam systems by March 2027.
AUSTRAC increases oversight of cryptocurrency exchanges and operators due to concerns about scam proceeds and money laundering.
AFP warns of scammers impersonating police claiming Operation Firestorm involvement, targeting cryptocurrency wallets through fake ReportCyber submissions.
ATO, AFP, and Services Australia raid alleged fraud syndicate using stolen identities and phishing to file fraudulent tax returns.
ASIC audit finds major gaps in scam protection communications at superannuation funds compared to banks.
AUSTRAC releases updated assessment warning of increasingly sophisticated money laundering and scam activity enabled by cryptocurrency and digital systems.
National Anti-Scam Centre reports Australians lost $2.18 billion to scams in 2025, an increase of 7.8% from 2024.
Australians lost over $837 million to investment scams in 2025, with investment fraud remaining a major threat across all demographics.
Fake cryptocurrency trading platforms targeting social media investment groups with fabricated profits and false trading data to steal deposits.
Job recruitment scams using fake task-based work from platforms like Amazon and YouTube, requesting upfront payments after small initial earnings.
Investment bonds scam where criminals impersonate legitimate businesses offering high-return environmental bonds with fake website and documentation.
SMSF illegal early access scams targeting vulnerable Australians through social media and cold calls, promising early superannuation withdrawals.
Business email compromise scam resulting in $600,000 loss detected through gold bullion purchases; three arrests made by NSW Police.
Federal government introduces mandatory scam prevention framework for banking, telecoms and digital platforms with automatic refund scheme for losses under $3,000.
Mobile phone number takeover fraud where scammers gain access to phone accounts to reset passwords and access banking and payment services.
Investment scams now account for nearly half of total scam losses, with median loss per victim reaching $7,000 compared to overall median of $400.
Financial crime landscape becoming more sophisticated with increased cryptocurrency-enabled money laundering and cross-border fraud operations.
Large-scale tax and welfare fraud syndicate dismantled; operation used stolen identities and phishing scams to access government accounts.
AI-powered investment scam websites and social media advertisements proliferating; ASIC removing record numbers of fraudulent phishing platforms.
Australians lost $2.18 billion to scams in 2025 with 481,523 reports; latest scam report reveals increasingly sophisticated and targeted fraud tactics.
Fake crypto trading platforms promoted through investment discussion groups via messaging apps with fabricated dashboards showing false profits.
Scammers impersonating ACCC phone numbers via spoofing to steal personal and financial information from Australians.
ASIC impersonation scams requesting payments in USD to release investor funds, using recovery scam tactics.
Scammers impersonating AFP and law enforcement claiming Operation Firestorm to extract cryptocurrency and seed phrases from victims.
SMS impersonation of major retailers and delivery companies with malicious links leading to credential-stealing websites.
AI-powered social media investment scam advertisements using celebrity impersonation and deepfakes to promote fraudulent schemes.
Mobile number takeover fraud enabling account access via verification code interception and password resets across banking and government services.
Recruiter impersonation scams offering work-from-home employment with flexible task-based opportunities and payments.
False bond investment scams impersonating ASIC and major banks offering unrealistic returns with guaranteed protection claims.
Qantas brand impersonation via email and SMS claiming reward points expiration, refunds, or gift claims with urgency tactics.
Voice cloning and deepfake AI-generated emergency calls from relatives requesting immediate cash transfers via social engineering.
Betting and sports investment scams accounting for tripled losses in 2025, often involving market manipulation and recovery tactics.

Mobile Number Takeover Targeting Financial Accounts Via SMS Interception. Criminals gain control of phone numbers or change telco account details, receiving 2FA codes and OTPs to compromise banking, myGov, subscriptions, and rewards programs. Joint ACCC-ACMA alert issued April 2026 following email/data breach compromises exposing identity documents and passwords.

Well-known Brand Impersonation via SMS for Task-Based Work Scams. Criminals spoof Amazon and YouTube via text messages, posing as recruiters offering well-paid, flexible work-from-home tasks to extract upfront fees or banking credentials. No legitimate organization delivers employment offers unsolicited via SMS.

Fake Crypto Trading Platforms Promoted in Messaging Groups. Scammers recruit victims from 'share trading' and 'stock tips' chat groups on messaging apps, redirecting to fraudulent cryptocurrency exchanges designed to steal deposit funds and harvest trading credentials for account takeovers.

AI-Powered Investment Scam Websites Surging at Record Takedown Rate. ASIC removed 11,964 phishing and investment scam websites in 2025 (90% increase), averaging 32 takedowns daily. AI-generated deepfakes impersonate celebrities and financial experts on paid social ads promoting fake trading platforms with fabricated performance dashboards.

Fake Investment Bonds Impersonating Real Businesses With Government Protection Claims. Criminals create near-identical broker emails and websites spoofing legitimate finance companies, offering high-return government-backed bonds (recently impersonating Bunnings promoting environmental bonds at 9% returns) requiring $50k-$250k deposits.

Fraudulent Letters Impersonating ASIC Demanding Fund Transfers. Scammers mail physical letters using authentic ASIC formatting and branding, falsely claiming bank staff under investigation and instructing victims to transfer 'asset protection fees' to secure accounts. ASIC never requests payments or transfers.

AI Voice-Cloning and Deepfake Video Scams Targeting Family Urgency. Criminals use artificial intelligence to clone family member voices in emergency calls ('Hi Mum') or create video deepfakes of relatives and celebrities, claiming urgent cash transfers needed. Australians lost $25.8 million to voice scams in first half of 2025.

Loan Application Scam Targeting Pacific Labour Mobility Workers. Criminals contact seasonal workers (PALM scheme) posing as loan assistants, charging application 'fees' and capturing bank access credentials. Once legitimate loans deposit, scammers transfer full funds to mule accounts, leaving victims with debt.

Fake Crypto Trading Platform Lures via Social Media Groups. Scammers target 'share trading' and 'stock tips' messaging app groups promoting fraudulent crypto trading platforms showing fake profits and trades. Targets retail investors aged 18-28 with high crypto exposure. Extraction: initial investment deposits which go directly to scammers; additional fake withdrawal fees extracted continuously. ASIC corroboration on May 25, 2026.

ACCC Phone Number Spoofing Impersonation Scheme. Criminals spoof publicly-listed ACCC phone numbers requesting sensitive banking information and passwords over phone. Some variants misuse 'ACCC' acronym or speak non-English languages to appear unrelated. Targets all Australians potentially in data breaches. Extraction: passwords, bank details, security question answers via telephone social engineering.

Fraudulent ASIC Impersonation Letters Claiming Staff Investigation. Fake letters mimicking ASIC branding falsely claim bank staff are under investigation and instruct recipients to transfer funds to 'secure accounts' to protect assets. Targets bank customers concerned about compliance. Extraction: bulk fund transfers under false pretense. No legitimate ASIC payment requests exist.

AFP/Police Impersonation Cryptocurrency Theft Scam. Scammers file false ReportCyber reports using victim data, then call victims impersonating AFP detectives under Operation Firestorm claiming data breach involvement. Targets cryptocurrency account holders. Extraction: full cryptocurrency transfers and seed phrase theft via authority intimidation.

Holiday Season Retail and Delivery Scam Surge. Fake retail sites mimicking established brands; fraudulent SMS about missed deliveries and unpaid fees directing to card-harvesting websites. Targets holiday shoppers. Extraction: payment card details via phishing sites claiming delivery issues. January-October 2025: 21,255 reports, $9.4M losses reported.

ATO DocuSign Impersonation Email Claiming Held Tax Refund. Scammers send fake 'DocuSign' emails claiming outstanding 'Declaration and Final Release' tax documents with subject lines mentioning notice of assessment. Targets taxpayers expecting refunds. Extraction: credentials via embedded fake myGov login pages when clicking Review Document button.

PALM Seasonal Worker Loan Assistance Scam. Scammers target Pacific Australia Labour Mobility program workers claiming to facilitate personal loans for upfront 'fees'. Victims often referred by co-workers who were themselves scammed. Extraction: banking credentials and direct fund theft after false loan approval. Victims unaware during transaction.

Physical Scratchie Prize Scam via Registered Post. Fake scratch-off lottery cards arrive by registered post claiming prizes require upfront payment fees. Exploits cost-of-living pressures with promises of travel giveaways and instant wins. Targets vulnerable households. Extraction: upfront processing and 'claim fees'. Physical delivery makes this novel scam vector.

AI-Generated Deepfake Celebrity Investment Ads on Sponsored Placements. Scammers use AI video manipulation and photo editing of prominent Australians (politicians, TV personalities, entrepreneurs) in sponsored social media ads claiming cryptocurrency/investment opportunities. Targets broad audience via Facebook/YouTube paid placements. Extraction: personal data and investment capital via malware sites or fraudulent platforms.

ASIC Alpha-Tag SMS Impersonation with Payment Demands. Fraudsters use SMS 'alpha tags' (text sender name spoofing) claiming to be ASIC, requesting payment to release frozen funds or assets. ASIC confirmed never uses alpha tags; credential to release funds is deceptive pretext extracting money from victims.

Fraudulent ASIC-Branded Email & Letter Scams Requesting Funds. Scammers send emails and physical letters using ASIC branding claiming bank staff under investigation, instructing transfers to 'secure accounts' to protect assets. ASIC does not request payments or account transfers; letters use authentic formatting to establish false legitimacy.

International Student 'Quick Cash' Account Takeover Scheme. Criminal syndicates approach departing international students offering hundreds of dollars for bank account and ID document access. Stolen identities used to open accounts; funds laundered via cryptocurrency. AFP warning issued late 2025; students at semester-end transition highest risk.

Travel QR Code & Fake Booking Confirmation Surge Ahead of Summer. Scammers overlay fake QR codes on legitimate ones at hotels/airports, send phishing messages impersonating airlines/booking platforms, use real booking details for fake payment requests. 'Reservation hijacking' exploits verification code interception; volume increasing with seasonal travel demand.

Fake Crypto Trading Platform Recruitment via Share Trading Groups. Scammers infiltrate Facebook/messaging share trading groups, invite members to 'exclusive' crypto asset trading platforms with fabricated credentials. Platforms harvest login credentials and direct investments to criminal wallets. Active Q1 2026; targets amateur investors.

Betting & Sports Investment Scam Surge ('Scambling'). Phishing ads on social media direct users to fake sports betting platforms offering guaranteed returns; 2025 saw $2.4M in reported losses—triple the prior year. Scammers use influencer impersonations and fake account histories to establish legitimacy.

Unlicensed Finfluencer Crackdown: Fake Financial Advice & Guaranteed Returns Claims. ASIC issued warning notices to four social media influencers providing unlicensed stock trading and crypto investment advice; reviewing 15 additional finfluencers under AFS licensee oversight. Part of Global Week of Action; targets Gen Z engagement via TikTok/Instagram.

AI-Enhanced Phishing & Deepfake Impersonation Scaling. Generative AI tools eliminating spelling/grammar tells in phishing emails; voice cloning and deepfake video enabling convincing executive impersonation for payment diversion. Technology improving speed of scam production; 2025 saw investment scams up 25% YoY exploiting AI-generated credibility.

Fake Crypto Trading Platform Scams via Messaging Apps Surge in June 2026. Scammers lure victims through social media 'share trading' or 'stock tips' groups, move conversations to WhatsApp/Telegram, impersonate financial influencers, and direct deposits to fake trading platforms that harvest funds directly. Gen Z disproportionately targeted for digital asset investment schemes.

ASIC Impersonation via Email and SMS Requesting Fund Transfers. Scammers send fraudulent communications claiming funds or assets are held in victim's name and requesting payment to release them. Emails spoof @asic.gov.au addresses; messages reference fake processing fees. Targets investment account holders and superannuation members.

Share Sale Fraud via Identity Theft Spike in 2026. Fraudsters compromise brokerage accounts through stolen credentials, execute unauthorized share transfers to external accounts, liquidate holdings. Brokers report clients detecting transfers after market execution. Targets retirement portfolio holders and investment account owners with six-figure holdings.

Sophisticated Fake Bond and Term Deposit Scams Using Legitimate Business Details. Scammers impersonate lesser-known financial services firms without digital footprint, mirror real ABNs and AFS licence numbers, create fake investor reviews, claim government deposit guarantee coverage. Victims directed to fake bank accounts or third-party transfers.

Fake AFP and ReportCyber Scam—Two-Stage Cryptocurrency Extortion. First scammer poses as AFP officer claiming victim involved in data breach, submits fraudulent ReportCyber report in victim's name with official reference numbers. Second scammer impersonates crypto platform demanding seed phrases or 'cold storage' fund transfers. Targets cryptocurrency account holders.

Loan Fee Scam Targeting PALM Scheme Workers. Fraudsters target Pacific Australia Labour Mobility workers with loan offers, charge upfront 'processing fees', gain access to banking credentials during application process. Once loan approved, scammers use victim's phone to transfer entire loan amount to their own accounts.

AI-Enhanced Phishing Emails Mask Grammar and Linguistic Red Flags. Fraudsters leverage generative AI to eliminate spelling/grammar errors that traditionally revealed phishing attempts. Emails impersonate trusted colleagues, contain sophisticated formatting, link to malware or credential harvesting domains. Affects corporate and institutional email users.

Deepfake Video and Photo Manipulation Scam Ads on Facebook/YouTube. Scammers create fake sponsored advertisements using manipulated video/images of Australian celebrities impersonating political figures. Clickbait headlines promote cryptocurrency schemes or fake wealth-generation products. Victims directed to malware or credential-harvesting sites.

PayID Exploitation in Facebook Marketplace Buy/Sell Transactions. Buyers on Facebook Marketplace request PayID payment method from sellers, forcing disclosure of phone number or email address linked to buyer's account. Compromised identity details used for follow-on fraud or account takeover.

Fake Crypto Trading Platform Recruitment: Scammers infiltrate messaging app investment groups and direct members to fraudulent crypto exchanges promising automated trading profits. Targets financially motivated users in share trading/stock tips communities seeking passive income.

SMS Impersonation of Major Retailers: Criminals spoof SMS from Amazon and YouTube to harvest credentials. Typically claims account action required or verification needed, directs targets to clone websites. Impacts broad consumer base relying on legitimate e-commerce platforms.

ACCC Phone Number Spoofing Scam: Scammers impersonate ACCC through voice calls using spoofed official numbers from the watchdog's public contact list. Requests personal/banking info citing urgent security matters or investigations. Exploits trust in government authority to extract credentials.

AI-Powered Investment Scam Surge: ASIC removed 11,964 scam websites in 2025—90% increase year-over-year. Scammers use AI deepfakes in social ads impersonating celebrities/officials, promote fake trading bots with unachievable returns, forge ASIC licensing claims. Targets retail investors via social media.

Fake Event Ticket Sales Surge in 2026: Scammers sell counterfeit or non-existent concert/sports/festival tickets on social media; Perth authorities warned of fake sky lantern drone show promotions. One-in-five Australians missed events after purchasing fake tickets in 2025.

Age Verification Scam Targeting Under-16 Social Media Users: Since December 2025 platform age restrictions, scammers impersonate TikTok/Facebook/Instagram claiming account suspension without age verification. Direct to fake form pages harvesting personal/financial data. Targets newly restricted youth demographic.

Telecom SIM Porting Fraud Enabling Account Takeovers: ACMA enforcement data shows Southern Phone Company failed identity checks 168 times during number ports, resulting in $393k consumer losses; Optus Mobile also fined. Compromised numbers enable account takeovers and credential theft across banking/crypto platforms.

Ghost Retailer E-commerce Fraud: Fake Australian e-commerce businesses ship poor-quality knock-offs from overseas or fail to deliver. Social media/search ads mimic legitimate sites with stolen branding. Targets bargain hunters and impulse online shoppers through ad networks.

2025 Scam Loss Surge Despite Report Stabilisation: Australian scam losses hit $2.18 billion across 481k+ reports in 2025—7.8% increase from 2024, median loss $400. Investment scams dominated at $837.7m; payment redirection and romance scams also surged. Federal government proposing $3k auto-refund scheme.
